title: Check yourself
questions.0.text: A form field returns the number of cups. The code is: const cups = form.value; console.log(cups + 1). The user typed 5. What comes out?
questions.0.options.3: An error, because text is added to a number
questions.0.why: Form fields always return text. "5" + 1 is not a sum — the plus sign joins text with a number and gives "51". Nothing breaks, which is what makes it dangerous.
questions.1.text: let total = 100; then a block with curly braces, and inside it var total = 0; After the block, total is:
questions.1.options.0: 100, because the block does not affect the outside
questions.1.options.2: The code breaks with a SyntaxError
questions.1.why: var does not stop at the edge of a block, but let has already taken the name at that level. Declaring it again with var is a clash, and the code breaks before it even starts.
questions.2.text: Which of these lines does NOT break?
questions.2.why: const stops the name from pointing at something else. But the thing itself can change from the inside — the array stays the same array. The other three each break a rule: reassigning a const, a const with no value, and one name declared twice.
questions.3.text: let a = 5; let b = 9; a = b; b = a; What does console.log(a, b) show?
questions.3.why: The first line makes a point at 9 and the five disappears — nothing is holding it any more. The second line takes the new value of a, which is 9 again. A swap needs a third name.
